title: A post-selected quantum model of cosmic acceleration
authors: Dimitris Lionas, Charis Anastopoulos, Konstantinos Gourgouliatos
abstract: The origin of cosmic acceleration remains a central problem in cosmology, commonly attributed to a cosmological constant within the $Λ$CDM model or to dynamical dark energy. Here, we develop an alternative approach in which acceleration emerges from quantum post-selection, a standard feature of quantum theory that is not usually incorporated into cosmological modelling. While quantum theory admits both pre-selected and post-selected ensembles, quantum cosmological models are almost exclusively formulated in terms of initial conditions. Building on previous work on post-selected quasiclassical dynamics, we construct a minimal predictive cosmological model in which post-selection and coarse-graining generate effective late-time acceleration without introducing a cosmological constant, dark energy, or modifications of general relativity. The resulting expansion history is highly constrained theoretically and depends on at most two parameters beyond standard Friedmann evolution. Confrontation with type Ia supernova and cosmic chronometer data yields statistically competitive fits while naturally avoiding the coincidence problem. The model also reproduces the standard radiation- and matter-dominated behaviour at early times and predicts a present-day jerk parameter significantly different from the $Λ$CDM value. These results suggest that cosmic acceleration may arise as a macroscopic quantum cosmological effect rather than from additional cosmological fluids or modified gravitational dynamics.
summary: This work presents a novel cosmological model where cosmic acceleration is explained not by dark energy, but as an emergent effect of quantum post-selection. By fitting this POQCO model against supernova and chronometer data, the authors claim to provide a theoretically grounded alternative to $\Lambda$CDM that naturally explains late-time acceleration.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental origin of cosmic acceleration, which is conventionally attributed to a cosmological constant or dark energy within the standard $\Lambda$CDM model.
main_result: It proposes that late-time cosmic acceleration can arise naturally from quantum post-selection effects in cosmology, yielding an expansion history consistent with current data without invoking new energy components or modifying General Relativity.
method: The authors develop a minimal predictive cosmological model based on Post-selected Quantum Cosmology (POQCO), deriving effective dynamics that are then constrained by fitting to Type Ia supernova and cosmic chronometer observational data.
model_or_system: The system is the Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker (FLRW) cosmology, analyzed using a quantum framework incorporating post-selection principles. The resulting effective evolution law is highly constrained, depending on at most two parameters beyond standard Friedmann dynamics.
key_observables: Cosmic acceleration history, present-day jerk parameter ($j_0$), and the Hubble function $H(z)$ derived from Pantheon+ supernovae and cosmic chronometer measurements.
important_parameters: The model is parameterized by constants related to post-selection ($\lambda$, $ eta$) and includes parameters like the effective sound-speed ($\kappa_0$) and the matter density parameter ($\Omega_m$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that early-time cosmic evolution reduces to standard radiation and matter domination, and it utilizes quantum post-selection as the source of late-time dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between POQCO predictions and $\Lambda$CDM for $H(z)$ (Hubble diagram), showing residuals relative to $\Lambda$CDM, and display posterior probability distributions from SNIa data.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem of cosmic acceleration, introduces the theoretical framework of Post-selected Quantum Cosmology (POQCO) derived from quantum principles, develops the effective cosmological equations, and finally performs a joint statistical analysis using both supernova and cosmic chronometer datasets to constrain model parameters.
why_interesting: While focused on cosmology, the core methodology—deriving macroscopic dynamics from fundamental quantum selection rules (post-selection)—is conceptually related to how complex emergent phenomena can arise from underlying quantum constraints, which has parallels in open quantum systems theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Post-selected cosmology and emergence of effective dynamics. (a) Post-selection: only histories with initial conditions in a region ∆i at time ti and final conditions in a re- gion ∆f at time tf contribute to the probabilities. (b) Coarse-graining: probabilities refer to phase-space tubes of characteristic size L. For fixed initial and final conditions, proba- bility concentrates on a single tube, while alternative histories are exponentially suppressed. (c) Comparison with standard evolution: the dynamics agrees with standard evolution at early times but deviates at late times as post-selection effects become significant.
